title: Where Susan Groseclose lived; dealing with homeless men; actives that Susan Groseclose did and dealings with her other family
description: Aunt Susan Groseclose. Her garden was by the railroad tracks. Finding a dead hobo. A hobo with lice who didn't want to leave their home, when only Ed and his mother were there; he acted as though he was a family friend. Aunt Susan was always busy; after she broke her hip, she crocheted all the time. She sat with Dixie's mother at night after working all day. She jumped over a horse by mistake. Susan's daughter made round button holes on her gown. Everyone called her "Aunt Susan."

title: Boys not knowing how to do any work in the home; Mr. Groseclose learning how to took; divisions of labor between men and women; Dixie's enjoyment of working outside
description: The boys in Ed's and Aunt Susan's families grew up without learning how to do any work in the home. He found himself ill-prepared to batch when he grew up: having to look after himself and the cattle when his sister got a toothache. His sister-in-law cooked her best to show him. The traditional division of labor between men and women. Dixie's great enjoyment of outside work and dislike of housework; her help with farming. She had trouble using a plow: "cat-and-covering".

title: Having to do housework and not liking it; missing school to help out at home; family illness
description: The work she had to do for the family as she was growing. Her preference for non-domestic work. She missed a great deal of school because of work at home; but she enjoyed school and always passed. She felt she was needed at home. Accepting what you have in life. Mother's illnesses.

title: Helping at home as a youngster. Putting up hay together for a farmer.
description: Helping at home as a youngster. Putting up hay together for a farmer.

title: Having tonsillitis and other health problems; growing up with adversity and making do with what they had
description: Her tonsillitis and other health problems after marriage. Growing from adversity: the children shared with each other. Most families had little. The "make-do" family.

title: No traveling to cities during the Depression; having produce was an advantage; cheap cost of cloth in the South
description: No point in going to cities during Depression; having produce was an advantage. Collecting from the railroad for killing their two cows. Cheap cost of cloth in the south; worked for seventy cents a week there.

title: Hand-me-downs and gifts of clothing for family; how her father made a living
description: Hand-me-downs and gifts of clothing for family. How her father made a living. Trapping - markets for skunk oil and gall.

title: Getting lice and having their horse and saddle stolen; Discussion of whites' relationship with Native Americans
description: A man who gave his brothers lice and stole their horse and saddle. John Smith robbed the Indians of corn, which set pattern of whites' forcing of Indians. Sorry record of whites' dealings with Indians. High Indian morals.

title: A Southerner who committed suicide after killing a black.
description: A Southerner who committed suicide after killing a black. Conscience catches up with murderers.

title: A cousin who was protected by God from killing a man with a gun, which misfired.
description: A cousin who was protected by God from killing a man with a gun, which misfired.

title: Survey dispute between his sister and Indians. Kimberling's land boundaries were set by himself, not a survey.
description: Survey dispute between his sister and Indians. Kimberling's land boundaries were set by himself, not a survey.

title: Native American friends teaching relatives English; Differences between Native Americans living on the land and whites
description: His Indian friend went to school and didn't like to speak Nez Perce, but taught his relatives English. A burial site for eight Nez Perces south of the river. 4n Indian family that was prone to tuberculosis because of their damp homesite. Indians could divide up land but whites couldn't. Indians might lie but wouldn't steal.

title: Dixie's poor treatment from Dr. Foster in Lewiston; Dr. Foster supposedly curing breast cancer
description: She got poor treatments in Lewiston from Dr. Foster, who was drinking heavily. A bootlegger who sold cold tea for liquor. Foster cured cancerous breast lumps by applying a burner, which was painful. A niece who died from breast cancer by dallying after it was discovered, in hope of getting Foster's cure.

title: Dr. Foster's house was haunted; Southern Superstitions; Jim Deevers' reputation as a wizard
description: Story that Foster's house was haunted by a figure of a nurse. Southern superstitions: throwing a sickle in milk that wouldn't churn; cure for breaking a spider web; killing an ox in an effort to cure it. Jim Deevers' reputation for being a wizard led to fun at others' expense, (continued)

title: Suspicion of the Deevers' family; religious beliefs about devil-worshippers
description: Suspicion of Jim Deevers; his wife Liz Deevers was called a witch. Ed's brother pretended to be a ghost on a roof. Devil-worshippers, a recent phenomenon, should be run down if they try to stop a person driving a car.

title: A superstitious Norwegian girl who helped the family out during , her sickness upset her.
description: A superstitious Norwegian girl who helped the family out during , her sickness upset her.

title: Dixie and Ed's courtship; what they did for fun; Dixie being prepared for married; and stepfather disapproval of Ed
description: Their courtship: when it started he was 20, she 13. His injury in an accident hauling poles led to his getting to know her better. As a little kid she once told Ed not to get married. Stepfather liked to talk about the wild times he had had, but disliked Dixie's going out. House parties: kissing games. She was prepared for marriage from work at home. Stepfather's disapproval of her beaus, including Ed. (continued)

title: Going to a dance and causing opposition for father; Dixie had no trouble getting dates; Going to Literaries
description: Taking Dixie to a dance raises opposition from father, although she got home by midnight with a chaperone. Stepfather had no legal right to stop her from marrying. Young men who were rounders became suspicious of their daughters. She had no trouble getting dates; some boys were too old to go out with. Going to literary with an overloaded buggy. Debate topics and other literary activities; mock trials, and the oath to lie.

title: 1918 Flu epidemic; home remedies for a variety of illnesses
description: Flu epidemic (1918) ended local gatherings. Treating flu; syrup of figs a good laxative. Powdered alum cured a mortal case of nosebleed.

title: Fire burned down their rented house at Teakean: trying to fight it.
description: Fire burned down their rented house at Teakean: trying to fight it.

title: Loss of belongings from fire except for saved produce in cellar; illnesses contracted from donated clothing; irresponsible doctor and regulations for ending quarantine
description: Loss of belongings in the fire; stored produce was saved in the cellar. Donations from neighbors - contracting the seven year itch from clothes they had been given. The children contracted scarlet fever. The doctor failed to come until they were on their way to Agatha, and then tried to stop them from moving. She protested so strongly that he let them go on. They had to be quarantined at Agatha; the store gave them credit but they couldn't sell their cream. Unreasonable regulations for ending quarantine; doctor's irresponsible conduct.

title: Loss of crops in 1928 due to bad weather and trying to farm at Teakean
description: Loss of crops that year because of bad weather (c. 1928). Effort to farm land at Teakean.

title: Brother was an English Lutheran minister; division in Ed's family about religion
description: Brother was an English Lutheran minister, strongly opposed to drinking and smoking. Division of Ed's family about religion.

title: Ed converting to religion; discussion of people in Russia
description: Hard luck in living. Ed has been converted to religion, which he didn't used to like. Under the czars, Russia had fine churches but the people were illiterate; improvement under communism. Dunkard practice of Lord's Supper.

title: Different interpretations of the Bible; first phonograph
description: Differing interpretations are made of Bible. Moral law originated in the Bible. The first phonograph he saw - the original invention was the important one.

title: Importance of religion to them as young people
description: Importance of religion to them as young people. Effects of "sanctification" may not show in the way a person conducts himself.

title: Adventists didn't work Saturdays but did on Sundays; Religious denominations are confusing to non-believers
description: Adventists at Arrow wouldn't help a man whose team got stuck on their Sabbath. An Adventist who hauled timber as the church got out on Sunday. Religious denominations are confusing to non-believers. An Adventist jailed for disturbing the peace.

title: Where Adams got his wheat and what he sold it for; Adams stopped shipping because his wheat was Egyptian; smuggling wheat to Kansas
description: Adams got his Alaska wheat from Lee Hall, who pawned it off on him as a yarn. Adams sold his wheat for $26 a bushel. Why Hall was miffed at Adams. Adams stopped from shipping his wheat once the government discovered it was Egyptian; smuggling his wheat to Kansas.

title: Man running for road boss which united Virginians; differences between North and South causes Methodist church split
description: A man ran for road boss to "beat the Southerners", causing the Virginians to unite. Leland's two Methodist churches, because of differences between North and South.

title: Getting drunk; writing poetry; acceptance of others
description: Getting drunk from booze and cigarettes. His writing of poetry. His acceptance of the needs of others; he didn't want to deprive others of work. Hoover gave money to the wealthy to hire people, but they didn't.

title: Roosevelt's program and the misuse of it in Lenore
description: Roosevelt's program. Misuse of program at Lenore - the man in charge hired his son as timekeeper and assistant foreman, but he didn't know how to do the job. A man who could do the work wouldn't.

title: Living in a section house.
description: Living in a section house.
